exercises.title: FAST Protocol Decoder
exercises.description: Decode binary FAST-protocol messages. FAST (FIX Adapted for STreaming) uses stop-bit encoding where each byte has 7 data bits and 1 stop bit (MSB). Implement the core decoding primitives: stop-bit integer decode, nullable field handling, presence map extraction, and full message decoding from a template. All arithmetic must use integers only -- no floating point.
exercises.instructions:
Implement the FAST protocol decoder with these functions:

1. `decode_stop_bit(data, offset)`: Decode a stop-bit encoded unsigned integer starting at byte offset. Each byte: MSB is stop bit (1=last byte), lower 7 bits are data. Return (decoded_value, next_offset).

2. `decode_signed_stop_bit(data, offset)`: Same as above but for signed integers. The first data bit of the first byte is the sign bit (1=negative). Negative values use sign extension.

3. `decode_pmap(data, offset)`: Decode the Presence Map (a stop-bit encoded bitmap). Return (pmap_bits_as_list, next_offset).

4. `decode_message(data, template)`: Decode a complete FAST message using a template. Template is a list of field descriptors: {'name': str, 'type': 'uint32'|'int32'|'string', 'optional': bool, 'operator': 'none'|'constant'|'copy'|'delta'|'increment', 'value': default_value}. Use the PMap to determine field presence. Return dict of field_name -> decoded_value.

5. `encode_stop_bit(value)`: Encode an unsigned integer using stop-bit encoding. Return list of bytes.

All values are integers. String fields use stop-bit encoded bytes where the last byte has MSB set.
exercises.starterCode:
class FASTDecoder:
    """FAST protocol decoder using stop-bit encoding."""
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.previous_values = {}  # for copy/delta/increment operators
    
    def encode_stop_bit(self, value: int) -> list:
        """Encode unsigned integer using stop-bit encoding.
        Returns list of byte values.
        """
        # TODO: Split value into 7-bit groups, set MSB of last byte
        pass
    
    def decode_stop_bit(self, data: list, offset: int) -> tuple:
        """Decode stop-bit encoded unsigned integer.
        Returns (value, next_offset).
        """
        # TODO: Read bytes until stop bit (MSB=1), assemble 7-bit chunks
        pass
    
    def decode_signed_stop_bit(self, data: list, offset: int) -> tuple:
        """Decode stop-bit encoded signed integer.
        Returns (value, next_offset).
        First data bit is sign bit.
        """
        # TODO
        pass
    
    def decode_pmap(self, data: list, offset: int) -> tuple:
        """Decode presence map (stop-bit encoded bitmap).
        Returns (list_of_bits, next_offset).
        """
        # TODO: Decode bytes, extract 7 data bits per byte as bit list
        pass
    
    def decode_string(self, data: list, offset: int) -> tuple:
        """Decode ASCII string (stop-bit encoded).
        Last byte has MSB set; strip it for the character.
        Returns (string, next_offset).
        """
        # TODO
        pass
    
    def decode_message(self, data: list, template: list) -> dict:
        """Decode a complete FAST message using template.
        
        template: list of {'name', 'type', 'optional', 'operator', 'value'}
        Returns dict of field_name -> decoded_value.
        """
        # TODO: Extract PMap, then decode each field per template
        pass
exercises.testCode:
dec = FASTDecoder()

# Test stop-bit encoding
assert dec.encode_stop_bit(0) == [0x80]
assert dec.encode_stop_bit(5) == [0x85]  # 5 = 0000101, with stop bit = 10000101
assert dec.encode_stop_bit(942) == [0x07, 0xAE]  # 942 = 0000111 0101110

# Test stop-bit decoding
val, off = dec.decode_stop_bit([0x85], 0)
assert val == 5 and off == 1

val, off = dec.decode_stop_bit([0x07, 0xAE], 0)
assert val == 942 and off == 2

# Test roundtrip
for test_val in [0, 1, 127, 128, 255, 1000, 65535, 1000000]:
    encoded = dec.encode_stop_bit(test_val)
    decoded, _ = dec.decode_stop_bit(encoded, 0)
    assert decoded == test_val, f"Roundtrip failed for {test_val}: got {decoded}"

# Test signed decoding
val, off = dec.decode_signed_stop_bit([0x85], 0)  # positive 5
assert val == 5

# Test PMap decoding
bits, off = dec.decode_pmap([0xC0], 0)  # 1100000 with stop bit
assert bits[0] == 1 and bits[1] == 1 and bits[2] == 0

# Test string decoding
val, off = dec.decode_string([0x41, 0x42, 0xC3], 0)  # "ABC"
assert val == "ABC"

# Test message decoding with template
template = [
    {'name': 'MsgType', 'type': 'string', 'optional': False, 'operator': 'constant', 'value': 'X'},
    {'name': 'SeqNum', 'type': 'uint32', 'optional': False, 'operator': 'none'},
    {'name': 'Price', 'type': 'uint32', 'optional': False, 'operator': 'none'},
]
# PMap: 11000000 with stop bit = 0xC0, then SeqNum=42, Price=1500
msg_data = [0xC0] + dec.encode_stop_bit(42) + dec.encode_stop_bit(1500)
result = dec.decode_message(msg_data, template)
assert result['MsgType'] == 'X'  # constant
assert result['SeqNum'] == 42
assert result['Price'] == 1500

print('All tests passed!')
hints: Stop-bit encoding: read bytes until you find one with MSB=1 (the stop bit). For each byte, the lower 7 bits are data. Accumulate by shifting left 7 and ORing: value = (value << 7) | (byte & 0x7F).
For encoding: split value into 7-bit groups from LSB to MSB. Reverse the groups. Set MSB of the last byte to 1 (stop bit). All other bytes have MSB=0.
The Presence Map (PMap) uses the same stop-bit encoding as integers, but you extract individual bits rather than assembling an integer. Each byte contributes 7 bits to the PMap.

exercises.title: Hardware Order Book
exercises.description: Implement a price-indexed array order book that mirrors how FPGAs maintain market state. Unlike software order books that use trees (O(log N)), this order book uses direct array indexing for O(1) access. ALL arithmetic must use integers (fixed-point) -- zero floating-point operations. Track best bid/ask, spread, book depth, and VWAP. Process Add, Modify, Cancel, and Execute events.
exercises.instructions:
Implement the `HardwareOrderBook` class.

Price representation: all prices are integers. price_int = price_dollars * 10000. For example, $150.25 = 1,502,500. Tick size for the instrument is given in the same units (e.g., 100 = $0.01).

1. `__init__(self, base_price_int, num_levels, tick_size_int)`: Initialize with a base price (integer), number of price levels per side, and tick size.
2. `add_order(self, side, price_int, qty)`: Add an order. Update level quantity and best bid/ask.
3. `cancel_order(self, side, price_int, qty)`: Cancel (reduce) quantity at a level. Update best bid/ask if the level becomes empty.
4. `execute_order(self, side, price_int, qty)`: Fill (execute) an order. Same as cancel but tracks trade statistics.
5. `modify_order(self, side, old_price_int, new_price_int, old_qty, new_qty)`: Price change = cancel at old + add at new.
6. Properties: `best_bid`, `best_ask`, `mid_price`, `spread`, `bid_depth` (total bid qty), `ask_depth`, `num_bid_levels` (count of non-empty bid levels), `num_ask_levels`.
7. `vwap(self, side, depth)`: VWAP for top `depth` levels. Return integer (fixed-point). Use integer arithmetic only.
8. `get_quote(self, position, base_half_spread, skew_coeff)`: Market-making quotes with inventory skew. Return (bid_price_int, ask_price_int) using ONLY integer arithmetic.

Constraint: NO floating-point operations anywhere. Verify by checking no use of float() or decimal division.
exercises.testCode:
TICK = 100  # $0.01 in 4-decimal fixed point
BASE = 1_500_000  # $150.00

book = HardwareOrderBook(BASE, num_levels=2000, tick_size_int=TICK)

# Test add orders
book.add_order('B', 1_502_500, 500)  # bid $150.25 x 500
book.add_order('B', 1_502_400, 300)  # bid $150.24 x 300
book.add_order('A', 1_502_600, 200)  # ask $150.26 x 200
book.add_order('A', 1_502_700, 150)  # ask $150.27 x 150

assert book.best_bid == 1_502_500
assert book.best_ask == 1_502_600
assert book.spread == 100  # 1 tick
assert book.mid_price == (1_502_500 + 1_502_600) >> 1  # integer mid
assert book.bid_depth == 800
assert book.ask_depth == 350
assert book.num_bid_levels == 2
assert book.num_ask_levels == 2

# Test cancel order -- best bid removed
book.cancel_order('B', 1_502_500, 500)
assert book.best_bid == 1_502_400  # next level becomes best
assert book.bid_depth == 300

# Test execute order
book.execute_order('A', 1_502_600, 100)
assert book.ask_depth == 250  # 200-100 + 150
assert book.total_trades == 1
assert book.total_trade_volume == 100

# Test modify order
book.add_order('B', 1_502_300, 200)
book.modify_order('B', 1_502_300, 1_502_500, 200, 250)
assert book.best_bid == 1_502_500  # modified to higher price

# Test VWAP (integer arithmetic)
bid_vwap = book.vwap('B', 3)
assert isinstance(bid_vwap, int)  # must be integer
assert bid_vwap > 0

# Test market-making quotes
bid_q, ask_q = book.get_quote(position=100, base_half_spread=50, skew_coeff=2)
assert isinstance(bid_q, int) and isinstance(ask_q, int)
assert ask_q > bid_q  # ask above bid
assert ask_q - bid_q > 100  # spread wider than base due to skew

# Verify NO floating point anywhere
import types
for name in dir(book):
    attr = getattr(book, name)
    if isinstance(attr, float):
        assert False, f"Float detected in attribute {name}"

print('All tests passed!')
hints: Price-to-index: idx = (price_int - base_price_int) // tick_size_int. This is a single integer subtraction and division -- in hardware, if tick_size is a power of 2 or 10, this simplifies to a shift or subtraction.
When the best bid level becomes empty after a cancel, scan downward (decrement index) until finding a non-empty level. In hardware, this would use a priority encoder; in Python, use a while loop.
For VWAP, accumulate sum(price * qty) and sum(qty) using integer multiplication and addition. The final division uses integer division (//) to stay in fixed-point. No floats needed.

exercises.title: Tick-to-Trade Latency Analyzer
exercises.description: Model each stage of the FPGA trading pipeline with clock cycle budgets. Given a trace of market events, compute per-stage and total latency, identify bottleneck stages, and compare with real-world published results (Boutros 270ns, IEEE 2022 433ns, IEEE 2024 480ns). Simulate pipeline parallelism where multiple packets process through different stages simultaneously.
exercises.instructions:
Implement the `LatencyAnalyzer` class.

1. `__init__(self, clock_freq_mhz)`: Initialize with FPGA clock frequency.
2. `add_stage(self, name, latency_cycles)`: Add a pipeline stage with its latency in clock cycles.
3. `analyze_single(self)`: Compute total latency for a single packet. Return dict with per-stage and total latency in nanoseconds.
4. `analyze_pipeline(self, num_packets)`: Simulate pipelined processing of multiple packets. With pipeline parallelism, new packets enter every max-stage-latency cycles. Return throughput (packets/sec) and per-packet latency.
5. `find_bottleneck(self)`: Return the stage with the highest latency (the pipeline bottleneck).
6. `compare_published(self)`: Compare your pipeline's latency against published results. Return a list of (paper, published_ns, your_ns, ratio) tuples.
7. `optimize_stage(self, name, new_latency_cycles)`: Reduce a stage's latency and show improvement.

Published reference points:
- Boutros et al. 2017: 270 ns average at 156.2 MHz
- IEEE 2022 system: 433 ns
- IEEE 2024 system: 480 ns
- Software baseline: 2000 ns (kernel bypass)
exercises.starterCode:

    """FPGA trading pipeline latency analysis tool."""
    
    PUBLISHED_RESULTS = [
        ('Boutros et al. 2017 (HLS)', 270),
        ('IEEE 2022 (Custom RTL)', 433),
        ('IEEE 2024 (FPGA accel)', 480),
        ('Software (kernel bypass)', 2000),
    ]
    
    def __init__(self, clock_freq_mhz: int = 200):
        self.clock_freq_mhz = clock_freq_mhz
        self.clock_period_ns = 1000 / clock_freq_mhz
        self.stages = []  # list of (name, latency_cycles)
    
    def add_stage(self, name: str, latency_cycles: int):

    def analyze_single(self) -> dict:

        Returns {'stages': [{name, cycles, ns}], 'total_cycles': N,
                 'total_ns': N, 'clock_freq_mhz': N}
        """

        pass
    
    def analyze_pipeline(self, num_packets: int) -> dict:
        """Analyze pipelined throughput.
        With pipeline parallelism, throughput = 1 packet per
        max-stage-latency cycles (the bottleneck determines throughput).
        Returns {'throughput_mpps': M packets/sec,
                 'bottleneck_stage': name,
                 'latency_ns': per-packet latency,
                 'total_time_ns': total for all packets}
        """
        # TODO
        pass
    
    def find_bottleneck(self) -> tuple:
        """Return (stage_name, latency_cycles, latency_ns) of bottleneck."""
        # TODO
        pass
    
    def compare_published(self) -> list:
        """Compare against published results.
        Returns [(paper, published_ns, your_ns, ratio)].
        """
        # TODO
        pass
    
    def optimize_stage(self, name: str, new_latency_cycles: int) -> dict:
        """Reduce a stage's latency. Return before/after comparison."""
        # TODO
        pass
exercises.testCode:
# Build a realistic FPGA trading pipeline
analyzer = LatencyAnalyzer(clock_freq_mhz=200)  # 200 MHz, 5ns period
analyzer.add_stage('NIC_RX', 2)        # 10 ns
analyzer.add_stage('NET_STACK', 3)     # 15 ns
analyzer.add_stage('FEED_DECODE', 5)   # 25 ns
analyzer.add_stage('BOOK_UPDATE', 7)   # 35 ns
analyzer.add_stage('STRATEGY', 9)      # 45 ns
analyzer.add_stage('RISK_CHECK', 3)    # 15 ns
analyzer.add_stage('ORDER_ENCODE', 10) # 50 ns
analyzer.add_stage('NIC_TX', 20)       # 100 ns

# Test single packet analysis
result = analyzer.analyze_single()
assert result['total_cycles'] == 59  # sum of all stages
assert result['total_ns'] == 295  # 59 * 5ns
assert len(result['stages']) == 8

# Test bottleneck identification
bottleneck = analyzer.find_bottleneck()
assert bottleneck[0] == 'NIC_TX'  # 20 cycles is the max
assert bottleneck[1] == 20

# Test pipeline analysis
pipeline = analyzer.analyze_pipeline(1000)
assert pipeline['bottleneck_stage'] == 'NIC_TX'
assert pipeline['throughput_mpps'] > 0
assert pipeline['latency_ns'] == 295

# Test comparison with published results
comparisons = analyzer.compare_published()
assert len(comparisons) == 4
for paper, pub_ns, my_ns, ratio in comparisons:
    assert my_ns == 295
    if 'Software' in paper:
        assert ratio < 1.0  # we should be faster than software

# Test optimization
opt = analyzer.optimize_stage('NIC_TX', 10)  # halve NIC_TX latency
assert opt['improvement_ns'] == 50  # saved 10 cycles * 5ns
assert opt['after_total_ns'] == 245
assert opt['improvement_pct'] > 0

print('All tests passed!')
hints: Single packet latency = sum of all stage latencies (they are sequential for one packet).
For pipelined throughput: the pipeline issues one result every `bottleneck_cycles` cycles after the initial fill. Total time = pipeline_latency + bottleneck_cycles * (N-1).
The bottleneck stage is the one with the maximum cycle count -- it determines the pipeline's throughput limit.

exercises.title: Fixed-Point Market-Making Strategy
exercises.description: Implement a market-making strategy that uses ONLY integer arithmetic -- zero floating-point operations. Quote bid and ask prices around the mid-price with inventory skew, process simulated fills, track position and P&L, and verify that no floating-point operations are used anywhere. This exercise directly maps to Project 5 Milestone 3.
exercises.instructions:
Implement the `FixedPointMarketMaker` class.

Price convention: all prices are integers with 4 decimal places. $150.25 = 1,502,500. Tick size = 100 ($0.01).

1. `__init__(self, base_spread_int, max_position, skew_per_lot)`: Initialize the market maker.
   - base_spread_int: minimum full spread in fixed-point units
   - max_position: maximum allowed position (absolute value)
   - skew_per_lot: how much to widen spread per unit of inventory

2. `compute_quotes(self, mid_price_int)`: Compute bid and ask prices. Apply inventory skew. Respect max position limits (do not quote the side that would breach limit). Return (bid_price, ask_price) or (0, ask_price) or (bid_price, 0) if position-limited.

3. `on_fill(self, side, price_int, qty)`: Process a fill. Update position, cost basis, and realized P&L.

4. `compute_pnl(self, mid_price_int)`: Compute realized and unrealized P&L (integers).

5. `simulate(self, mid_prices, fill_probability)`: Run simulation over a series of mid-price updates. At each step, compute quotes. With given probability, simulate a fill on each side. Return statistics dict.

6. `verify_no_floats(self)`: Verify that all internal state is integer. Return True if no floats found.

Constraint: ALL arithmetic must be integer. Use // for division, >> for halving, * for multiplication. NO float(), no decimal points in arithmetic.
exercises.starterCode:

exercises.solutionCode:
class FixedPointMarketMaker:
    TICK = 100
    
    def __init__(self, base_spread_int: int, max_position: int,
                 skew_per_lot: int):
        self.base_spread = base_spread_int
        self.max_position = max_position
        self.skew_per_lot = skew_per_lot
        self.position = 0
        self.total_cost = 0  # sum of price*qty for buys (negative for sells)
        self.total_qty_bought = 0
        self.total_qty_sold = 0
        self.realized_pnl = 0
        self.num_trades = 0
    
    def compute_quotes(self, mid_price_int: int) -> tuple:
        half_spread = self.base_spread >> 1  # integer division by 2
        
        # Inventory skew: widen spread on the side with inventory
        skew = self.skew_per_lot * self.position  # positive if long
        
        bid_price = mid_price_int - half_spread - skew
        ask_price = mid_price_int + half_spread + skew
        
        # Ensure ask > bid (skew can flip if position is extreme)
        if ask_price <= bid_price:
            mid = (bid_price + ask_price) >> 1
            bid_price = mid - self.TICK
            ask_price = mid + self.TICK
        
        # Position limits
        if self.position >= self.max_position:
            bid_price = 0  # don't buy more
        if self.position <= -self.max_position:
            ask_price = 0  # don't sell more
        
        return (bid_price, ask_price)
    
    def on_fill(self, side: str, price_int: int, qty: int):
        self.num_trades += 1
        
        if side == 'B':
            # Buying: position increases
            self.total_cost += price_int * qty
            self.total_qty_bought += qty
            self.position += qty
        else:
            # Selling: position decreases
            # Realize P&L based on average cost
            if self.total_qty_bought > 0 and self.position > 0:
                avg_cost = self.total_cost // self.position if self.position > 0 else price_int
                self.realized_pnl += (price_int - avg_cost) * qty
            self.total_qty_sold += qty
            self.position -= qty
        
        # Reset cost basis if position crosses zero
        if self.position == 0:
            self.total_cost = 0
        elif self.position > 0 and self.total_qty_bought > 0:
            # Maintain average cost for remaining position
            if self.total_qty_bought > self.total_qty_sold:
                pass  # keep running total_cost
    
    def compute_pnl(self, mid_price_int: int) -> dict:
        if self.position > 0 and self.total_cost > 0:
            avg_cost = self.total_cost // self.position
            unrealized = (mid_price_int - avg_cost) * self.position
        elif self.position < 0:
            unrealized = 0  # simplified for short
        else:
            unrealized = 0
        
        return {
            'realized_pnl': self.realized_pnl,
            'unrealized_pnl': unrealized,
            'total_pnl': self.realized_pnl + unrealized,
            'position': self.position
        }
    
    def simulate(self, mid_prices: list, fill_probability_pct: int) -> dict:
        # Deterministic LCG random number generator (integer only)
        lcg_state = 12345
        lcg_a = 1103515245
        lcg_c = 12345
        lcg_m = 1 << 31
        
        def next_random():
            nonlocal lcg_state
            lcg_state = (lcg_a * lcg_state + lcg_c) % lcg_m
            return lcg_state % 100  # returns 0-99
        
        total_quotes = 0
        total_fills = 0
        max_position = 0
        min_position = 0
        
        for mid in mid_prices:
            bid, ask = self.compute_quotes(mid)
            total_quotes += 1
            
            # Simulate bid fill
            if bid > 0 and next_random() < fill_probability_pct:
                self.on_fill('B', bid, 1)
                total_fills += 1
            
            # Simulate ask fill
            if ask > 0 and next_random() < fill_probability_pct:
                self.on_fill('S', ask, 1)
                total_fills += 1
            
            if self.position > max_position:
                max_position = self.position
            if self.position < min_position:
                min_position = self.position
        
        final_pnl = self.compute_pnl(mid_prices[-1] if mid_prices else 0)
        
        return {
            'total_quotes': total_quotes,
            'total_fills': total_fills,
            'final_position': self.position,
            'max_position': max_position,
            'min_position': min_position,
            'realized_pnl': final_pnl['realized_pnl'],
            'total_pnl': final_pnl['total_pnl'],
            'num_trades': self.num_trades
        }
    
    def verify_no_floats(self) -> bool:
        for attr_name in ['position', 'total_cost', 'total_qty_bought',
                          'total_qty_sold', 'realized_pnl', 'num_trades',
                          'base_spread', 'max_position', 'skew_per_lot']:
            val = getattr(self, attr_name)
            if isinstance(val, float):
                return False
        return True
exercises.testCode:
# Test basic quoting
mm = FixedPointMarketMaker(
    base_spread_int=200,  # $0.02 spread
    max_position=100,
    skew_per_lot=2        # 2 units per lot of skew
)

# Quotes with no position
bid, ask = mm.compute_quotes(1_502_500)  # mid = $150.25
assert bid > 0 and ask > 0
assert ask > bid
assert ask - bid >= 200  # at least base spread

# Process a buy fill
mm.on_fill('B', 1_502_400, 10)  # buy 10 at $150.24
assert mm.position == 10

# Quotes should now be skewed (wider on bid side due to long position)
bid2, ask2 = mm.compute_quotes(1_502_500)
assert bid2 < bid  # bid should be lower (discouraging more buys)
assert ask2 > ask  # ask should be higher

# Test position limit
mm2 = FixedPointMarketMaker(200, max_position=5, skew_per_lot=2)
for i in range(5):
    mm2.on_fill('B', 1_502_400, 1)
assert mm2.position == 5
bid, ask = mm2.compute_quotes(1_502_500)
assert bid == 0  # should not quote bid when at max position
assert ask > 0   # should still quote ask

# Test P&L computation
mm3 = FixedPointMarketMaker(200, max_position=100, skew_per_lot=2)
mm3.on_fill('B', 1_502_400, 10)  # buy 10 at $150.24
pnl = mm3.compute_pnl(1_502_600)  # mid now $150.26
assert pnl['position'] == 10
assert isinstance(pnl['realized_pnl'], int)
assert isinstance(pnl['unrealized_pnl'], int)
assert isinstance(pnl['total_pnl'], int)

# Test no floats
assert mm3.verify_no_floats() == True

# Test simulation
mm4 = FixedPointMarketMaker(200, max_position=50, skew_per_lot=1)
mid_prices = [1_502_500 + (i % 20 - 10) * 100 for i in range(100)]  # oscillating
stats = mm4.simulate(mid_prices, fill_probability_pct=30)
assert stats['total_quotes'] == 100
assert stats['total_fills'] > 0
assert stats['num_trades'] > 0
assert isinstance(stats['realized_pnl'], int)
assert abs(stats['final_position']) <= 50  # within limits

print('All tests passed!')
hints: For integer halving, use >> 1 (right-shift by 1). This is free in hardware (just rewiring).
For the LCG random number generator, use the classic formula: state = (a * state + c) % m where a=1103515245, c=12345, m=2^31. All integer arithmetic.
Track cost basis as total_cost (sum of price * qty for buys). Average cost = total_cost // position (integer division). Realized P&L on a sell = (sell_price - avg_cost) * qty.
